criterion_breakdown.label: Shell-law fidelity
criterion_breakdown.note: Best fit because it keeps shell grammars distinct in code and deployables while still sharing contract infrastructure.

criterion_breakdown.label: Cross-shell code sharing without semantic drift
criterion_breakdown.note: Shared packages cover the contract spine, while separate apps keep portal and internal shell composition from collapsing together.

criterion_breakdown.label: Deployable isolation between operator/governance and portal surfaces
criterion_breakdown.note: Portal stays physically separate from internal operator/governance bundles and session posture.

criterion_breakdown.label: Route continuity and deep-link stability
criterion_breakdown.note: One shared route runtime can preserve same-object continuity across both deployables without route-family seams inside a page.

criterion_breakdown.label: Auth/session integration fit
criterion_breakdown.note: Separate deployables let portal and internal sessions enforce different browser posture while still using the same IdP and handoff contracts.

criterion_breakdown.label: Customer-safe separation
criterion_breakdown.note: This is the cleanest way to keep portal copy, selectors, and bundles customer-safe by default.

criterion_breakdown.label: Performance and bundle isolation
criterion_breakdown.note: Each audience ships only the shell families it needs, with route-level splitting inside each deployable.

criterion_breakdown.label: Design-token and selector reuse
criterion_breakdown.note: Shared token and selector packages keep browser implementation consistent without forcing identical visual grammar.

criterion_breakdown.label: Testability with Playwright
criterion_breakdown.note: Two deployables plus one selector grammar keeps tests deterministic and clear about audience boundaries.

criterion_breakdown.label: Evolvability for later phase-05 implementation tasks
criterion_breakdown.note: Later teams get clean app/package seams and can implement shells without re-deriving deployable doctrine.

criterion_breakdown.label: Operational simplicity and blast-radius control
criterion_breakdown.note: Two deployables add some operational work, but the blast-radius and bundle safety payoff is high.

alternatives.label: Shared TypeScript/React web platform with two deployables
risks: Requires disciplined shared-package governance so shell-specific design and copy do not drift into one another.
strengths: Preserves portal deployable isolation while keeping one shared contract spine.
Allows calm-shell and governance surfaces to share the internal session and route runtime without shipping that bundle to portal users.
alternatives.summary: Build one shared browser platform with shared contracts, tokens, route runtime, selector grammar, API clients, and continuity logic, but ship two deployables: `operator-web` and `client-portal-web`.

criterion_breakdown.label: Shell-law fidelity
criterion_breakdown.note: It can preserve shell law in theory, but one runtime increases pressure to normalize all shells into one dashboard grammar.

criterion_breakdown.label: Cross-shell code sharing without semantic drift
criterion_breakdown.note: Code sharing is easy, but the same convenience makes semantic bleed between portal and internal shells more likely.

criterion_breakdown.label: Deployable isolation between operator/governance and portal surfaces
criterion_breakdown.note: Portal and internal code inevitably co-reside, making audience isolation weaker and blast radius wider.

criterion_breakdown.label: Route continuity and deep-link stability
criterion_breakdown.note: A single runtime can preserve route history well, which is its strongest point.

criterion_breakdown.label: Auth/session integration fit
criterion_breakdown.note: One mega app has to juggle materially different audience/session postures inside one runtime envelope.

criterion_breakdown.label: Customer-safe separation
criterion_breakdown.note: Customer-safe separation depends too heavily on runtime branching and discipline rather than build-level isolation.

criterion_breakdown.label: Performance and bundle isolation
criterion_breakdown.note: Portal users are more exposed to unnecessary internal code and styling churn even with code splitting.

criterion_breakdown.label: Design-token and selector reuse
criterion_breakdown.note: One app does make shared tokens and selectors easy to centralize.

criterion_breakdown.label: Testability with Playwright
criterion_breakdown.note: Tests stay in one runtime, but audience-specific regressions become more coupled and noisy.

criterion_breakdown.label: Evolvability for later phase-05 implementation tasks
criterion_breakdown.note: Later teams get a simpler repo picture, but fewer safe boundaries for independent implementation and rollout.

criterion_breakdown.label: Operational simplicity and blast-radius control
criterion_breakdown.note: Operationally simple on paper because there is only one browser deployable.

alternatives.label: One single mega web application containing all shell families and audiences
risks: Weakens portal isolation and makes bundle/code leakage harder to control.
strengths: Simplifies some deployment plumbing and can make local development feel more centralized.
Keeps all shells under one runtime process and one build graph.
alternatives.summary: Ship one browser application for every audience and shell family, with route guards and runtime branching for portal, operator, and governance surfaces.

criterion_breakdown.label: Shell-law fidelity
criterion_breakdown.note: Route-level seams and cross-bundle composition make exact shell continuity and support-region law harder to preserve.

criterion_breakdown.label: Cross-shell code sharing without semantic drift
criterion_breakdown.note: Each micro-frontend wants its own primitives and runtime conventions, which weakens the shared shell law spine.

criterion_breakdown.label: Deployable isolation between operator/governance and portal surfaces
criterion_breakdown.note: Isolation is strong, which is the main appeal of this option.

criterion_breakdown.label: Route continuity and deep-link stability
criterion_breakdown.note: Deep-link restore, browser back, and support-region focus return are hardest when route ownership crosses runtime seams.

criterion_breakdown.label: Auth/session integration fit
criterion_breakdown.note: Browser handoff and session transitions become more complex when many runtimes can own the same user journey.

criterion_breakdown.label: Customer-safe separation
criterion_breakdown.note: Portal can be isolated, but the cost is more cross-app contract stitching for shared customer-request flows.

criterion_breakdown.label: Performance and bundle isolation
criterion_breakdown.note: Bundle isolation can be good, though runtime orchestration overhead offsets some of that benefit.

criterion_breakdown.label: Design-token and selector reuse
criterion_breakdown.note: Selector grammar and token discipline are the first things to drift in a micro-frontend layout.

criterion_breakdown.label: Testability with Playwright
criterion_breakdown.note: End-to-end tests become more brittle once shell continuity crosses multiple independently mounted runtimes.

criterion_breakdown.label: Evolvability for later phase-05 implementation tasks
criterion_breakdown.note: Ownership looks flexible, but later implementation has to solve shell drift and continuity tax repeatedly.

criterion_breakdown.label: Operational simplicity and blast-radius control
criterion_breakdown.note: Many deployables and seams increase operational overhead significantly.

alternatives.label: Micro-frontend decomposition by route family, shell, or feature slice
risks: Shell continuity, selector grammar, and focus restoration become materially harder to keep exact across seams.
strengths: Can isolate ownership aggressively and shrink individual feature bundles.
Looks attractive when many teams want independent shipping cadence.
alternatives.summary: Split manifest, collaboration, portal, and governance surfaces into separate micro-frontends composed at runtime.

criteria.label: Shell-law fidelity
criteria.rationale: The topology must preserve the three shell families and their distinct layout grammars without collapsing them into one generic dashboard.

criteria.label: Cross-shell code sharing without semantic drift
criteria.rationale: The shared web platform must reuse contracts, tokens, selector grammar, and continuity logic without forcing one shell grammar onto another.

criteria.label: Deployable isolation between operator/governance and portal surfaces
criteria.rationale: Internal operator/governance code and customer portal code need clear deployable boundaries for audience safety, bundle isolation, and blast-radius control.

criteria.label: Route continuity and deep-link stability
criteria.rationale: Refresh, deep link, back/return, and notification open must preserve the same owned shell and object context where the corpus requires it.

criteria.label: Auth/session integration fit
criteria.rationale: Browser handoff, step-up, and session posture must map cleanly to the chosen deployable split.

criteria.label: Customer-safe separation
criteria.rationale: Portal surfaces must remain physically and semantically isolated from staff-only semantics and hidden internal context.

criteria.label: Performance and bundle isolation
criteria.rationale: The browser topology should allow route-level code splitting and avoid shipping internal operator/governance code to portal users.

criteria.label: Design-token and selector reuse
criteria.rationale: Shared tokens, semantic selectors, and interaction-layer bindings need one platform spine so later implementation does not drift.

criteria.label: Testability with Playwright
criteria.rationale: The topology should encourage semantic locators, deterministic shells, and stable browser automation contracts.

criteria.label: Evolvability for later phase-05 implementation tasks
criteria.rationale: The chosen topology must give later web implementation tasks stable package seams, deployable seams, and testing seams.

criteria.label: Operational simplicity and blast-radius control
criteria.rationale: The deployable split should stay operationally coherent while still limiting blast radius between internal and portal surfaces.
